text: The camera view shows a large indirect right inguinal hernia, with visible protrusion in the inguinal region.
The surgeon examines a large, chronically incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia with visible sliding characteristics and attachments to the cecal suspensory ligament.
The surgeon removes scar tissue and dissects the small bowel mesentery, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon is freeing up tissue adhesions around the chronically incarcerated indirect renal hernia, carefully separating the structures involved.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia but decides to take down the hernia sac first, indicating the visible presence of the hernia sac in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, ensuring that the flap comes down as part of the dissection process.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection on a large hernia, followed by a medial dissection, before focusing on reducing the indirect hernia sac.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and begins working medially, focusing on the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates into the space of Retzius, identifying Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle. The video then jumps forward, showing the completed dissection on the left side.
The surgeon dissects medially, taking down the bladder and surrounding tissues.
The video shows the fully dissected space of Retzius on the left side of the screen, with clear visualization of the anatomical area.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the bowel, avoiding pulling, banging, or cauterizing it while it remains positioned within the surgical field.
The inferior epigastric vessels are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates through dense scar tissue, staying close to the previously identified inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon has sufficiently dissected and loosened the chronically scarred hernia sac, allowing the assistant at the bedside to reduce the hernia contents.
The hernia sac is visibly releasing as the surgeon manipulates the surrounding tissue, indicating successful reduction of the hernia.
The surgeon applies tension with the left hand, likely to aid in manipulating or positioning tissues during the procedure.
The surgeon uses cautery to gently touch the tissues, causing them to visibly separate.
The surgeon uses a cautery device to cauterize, cut, and push tissue, performing repetitive actions to manipulate and separate the surgical field.
The surgeon successfully releases the indirect hernia sac, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon addresses the final scar tissue located along the epigastric vessels, likely using a surgical instrument to manipulate or remove it.
The surgeon continues the dissection and finally extracts the entire sac, using a combination of cauterization and cutting techniques to separate the tissues.
The surgeon cleans up the medial dissection area, ensuring clear visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection until the obturator canal becomes visible, indicating progress in removing the indirect sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the retroperitoneum and gonadal vessels, ensuring adequate space for the placement of a large mesh.
The surgeon rolls up the mesh and places it inside a half-inch Penrose drain, which fits easily.
The assistant at the bedside inserts the rolled-up mesh, contained within a Penrose drain, into the abdominal cavity using a Kelly clamp.
The surgeon makes a small slit and easily pulls the mesh out of the Penrose drain, which is then removed at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon displays an extra large mesh, likely prepared for insertion or placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon displays a large mesh, highlighting the noticeable difference in size compared to other meshes used in the procedure.
The surgeon begins tacking the mesh into place, securing it to the surrounding tissue and connecting the mesh pieces together.
The surgeon places additional stitches on the right side to secure the mesh in position.
The surgeon checks to ensure that the mesh is laying flat after closing the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon closes the left side peritoneum, completing the suturing of this anatomical area.
The left side flap appears decompressed due to the presence of an angiocath inserted into the area.
The surgeon checks the left side to ensure the flap is laying flat after decompression.
On the right side, an enormous indirect hernia sac is visible.
The surgeon places a vicryl suture to imbricate the large indirect hernia sac, sewing to reduce its size and secure it in place.
The surgeon manipulates the hernia sac to prevent the bowel from herniating into it, ensuring the sac is properly managed.
The surgeon observes the lateral aspect of the right-sided extra-large mesh, noting that it is folding up during the procedure.
The surgeon pulls up on the peritoneum and pushes it back down, flattening it over the hernia sac.
The surgeon incises directly on the flap, creating a small hole.
The surgeon identifies a single band of lateral attachment still connected to the tissue.
The surgeon observes that pulling up and closing the peritoneal flap causes the lateral aspect of the mesh to fold up.
The surgeon uses cautery to remove the remaining lateral attachment, allowing the mesh to lay flat against the tissue.
The surgeon finalizes the closure of the peritoneum, ensuring that the mesh lays flat across the surgical area.
